You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection.

## Context ## 
Users reported an optimization bug in LLVM. Maintainers provided a fix, but fixes often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task ##
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive ##
- Original bug description and failing test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the fix)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use ##

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls: 
- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `langref`: Query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `stop`: End Phase 1 by submitting the identified issues and proposed test strategies.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.
   
You should pay special attention to `stop` and `report` tools, which are used to end Phase 1 and Phase 2 respectively.

# Phase 1: Analyze the Fix

This is a patch for fixing miscompilation bugs in LoopVectorize: 

commit 9ee8e38cdcc6925a4127d44a0360dc8de23dfb5f
Author: Florian Hahn <flo@fhahn.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 26 21:29:42 2024 +0100

    [VPlan] Also propagate versioned strides to users via sext/zext.
    
    The versioned value may not be used in the loop directly but through a
    sext/zext. Add new live-ins in those cases.

diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorize.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorize.cpp
index 33c4decd58a6..74ceb9eecf21 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorize.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorize.cpp
@@ -8817,12 +8817,24 @@ LoopVectorizationPlanner::tryToBuildVPlanWithVPRecipes(VFRange &Range) {
     // Only handle constant strides for now.
     if (!ScevStride)
       continue;
-    Constant *CI = ConstantInt::get(Stride->getType(), ScevStride->getAPInt());
 
-    auto *ConstVPV = Plan->getOrAddLiveIn(CI);
-    // The versioned value may not be used in the loop directly, so just add a
-    // new live-in in those cases.
-    Plan->getOrAddLiveIn(StrideV)->replaceAllUsesWith(ConstVPV);
+    auto *CI = Plan->getOrAddLiveIn(
+        ConstantInt::get(Stride->getType(), ScevStride->getAPInt()));
+    if (VPValue *StrideVPV = Plan->getLiveIn(StrideV))
+      StrideVPV->replaceAllUsesWith(CI);
+
+    // The versioned value may not be used in the loop directly but through a
+    // sext/zext. Add new live-ins in those cases.
+    for (Value *U : StrideV->users()) {
+      if (!isa<SExtInst, ZExtInst>(U))
+        continue;
+      VPValue *StrideVPV = Plan->getLiveIn(U);
+      if (!StrideVPV)
+        continue;
+      VPValue *CI = Plan->getOrAddLiveIn(ConstantInt::get(
+          U->getType(), ScevStride->getAPInt().getSExtValue()));
+      StrideVPV->replaceAllUsesWith(CI);
+    }
   }
 
   VPlanTransforms::dropPoisonGeneratingRecipes(*Plan, [this](BasicBlock *BB) {
diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/VPlan.h b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/VPlan.h
index 21b088cd2386..71387bf5b7e9 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/VPlan.h
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/VPlan.h
@@ -3215,6 +3215,9 @@ public:
     return Value2VPValue[V];
   }
 
+  /// Return the live-in VPValue for \p V, if there is one or nullptr otherwise.
+  VPValue *getLiveIn(Value *V) const { return Value2VPValue.lookup(V); }
+
 #if !defined(NDEBUG) || defined(LLVM_ENABLE_DUMP)
   /// Print the live-ins of this VPlan to \p O.
   void printLiveIns(raw_ostream &O) const;


## Your Task for Phase 1 ##
   
Analyze the fix above carefully and identify potential issues, edge cases, or gaps that the fix might miss. According to the analysis, propose test strategies for exposing the issue. These strategies will be used in Phase 2 to mutate existing test cases to target the identified issues.
   
Please notice that you are analyzing the fix proposed by an expert LLVM developer. So you should pay special attention to the semantics of the fix, especially regarding deep optimization correctness, rather than simple syntax or surface-level issues. 
   
## Subsystem Knowledge for LoopVectorize ##

For reference, here are some key points about the LoopVectorize you should consider (but do not limit to these) in your analysis:

# Subsystem Knowledge for LoopVectorize

## Elements Frequently Missed

*   **Outside Users of Loop-Defined Values (LCSSA Phi Nodes)**: The uniformity analysis frequently misses or ignores users outside the loop when determining if an instruction is uniform. This leads to incorrect scalarization where the vectorizer extracts from the first lane (lane 0) instead of the last active lane required by the outside user.
*   **Poison Generation in Speculated Lanes**: The vectorizer misses the implications of evaluating potentially undefined operations (like out-of-bounds shifts) in inactive or speculated vector lanes. When these `poison` values feed into vector boolean reductions (e.g., `llvm.vector.reduce.or`) for early exits, the `poison` propagates and corrupts the entire control flow.
*   **Internal SCEV Cache References**: Invalidation mechanisms frequently miss internal Scalar Evolution (SCEV) cache references (such as Backedge Taken Counts) when loop transformations (like peeling or vectorization) modify the Control Flow Graph. The invalidation only traverses IR use-def chains, leaving stale, invalid expressions attached to LCSSA Phi nodes.
*   **Scalar Vectorization Factors (VF=1) with Interleaving**: The internal logic for identifying the first active lane and extracting live-out values misses support for cases where the vectorization factor is 1 but the interleave count is greater than 1. This causes the compiler to bypass necessary extraction steps for early exits.
*   **Dead or Unreachable Control Flow Paths in Structural Checks**: During the VPlan code generation phase, structural invariance checks miss the broader context of dead or unreachable control flow paths that advanced analyses (like SCEV) successfully navigate. This causes loop-invariant values to be incorrectly treated as varying vectors.

## Patterns Not Well Handled

### Pattern 1: Live-Out Value Extraction and Lane Selection
The loop vectorizer struggles significantly with extracting the correct scalar value from a vector for uses outside the loop (live-outs). This pattern manifests in several ways depending on the analysis applied. If uniformity analysis incorrectly flags an induction update as uniform because it ignores outside users, the vectorizer extracts from lane 0 instead of the final active lane. If the cost model and code generation phases disagree on whether a value is loop-invariant (advanced SCEV analysis vs. structural VPlan checks), the vectorizer may attempt to extract the last lane of a varying vector that actually contains garbage data from a dead control flow path. Furthermore, when interleaving is applied without vectorization (VF=1, IC>1), the vectorizer completely fails to generate the logic required to identify which interleaved part triggered an early exit, leading to the extraction of an incorrect live-out value.

### Pattern 2: Speculative Evaluation of Uncountable Early Exits
Loops with uncountable early exits (e.g., data-dependent breaks) require the vectorizer to evaluate exit conditions concurrently across multiple lanes and reduce the results. This high-level pattern is poorly handled because it inherently involves speculative execution of iterations that would normally be skipped in scalar execution. If the exit condition involves operations that can produce `poison` (such as an oversized bit shift on data from a later, unreachable iteration), this `poison` is injected into the vector lanes. Because the vectorizer uses bitwise reductions (like `llvm.vector.reduce.or`) to collapse the lane conditions into a single scalar branch condition, a single `poison` lane contaminates the entire reduction. This results in undefined behavior and miscompilation of the loop's control flow.

### Pattern 3: State and Analysis Inconsistencies Across Compiler Phases
A recurring pattern of failure arises from discrepancies between different analysis and transformation phases within the compiler pipeline. The optimization pass often relies on powerful, advanced analyses (like SCEV) to make initial cost and predication decisions, but falls back on weaker, structural checks during actual code generation (VPlan). For example, SCEV might prove a stored value is loop-invariant despite complex PHI nodes, prompting the vectorizer to plan a uniform unconditional store. However, VPlan's structural checks reject the invariance, forcing a varying vector extraction that yields incorrect data. Similarly, when loop transformations alter the CFG by adding predecessors to exit blocks, they fail to thoroughly invalidate the advanced SCEV state. This leaves a dangerous inconsistency where subsequent passes query SCEV and receive stale, mathematically incorrect expressions for LCSSA Phi nodes, leading to infinite loops or incorrect trip counts.
   
Once you have analyzed the fix and identified potential issues or edge cases, use the `stop` tool to specify the issues you found and proposed test strategies along with detailed reasoning. Follow this structure: 

1. **Fix Understanding**: 
   - What optimization or transformation does this fix enable or correct?
   - What are the key changes made in the patch?

2. **Assumptions Identified**:
   - What assumptions does the fix make about the input?
   - What preconditions must hold for this fix to be correct?

3. **Potential Cases to Break Assumptions**:
For each assumption and precondition identified, describe specific scenarios or input characteristics that could violate them. The potential cases should only focus on the semantics related to the analysis above.
   
4. **Test Strategies**:
   For each potential issue you identified, propose specific test strategies:
   - **Strategy #1**: [Name]
   - Target: [What to mutate]
   - Rationale: [Why this might expose an issue]
   - Expected Issue: [What incorrect behavior might occur]
   
   - **Strategy #2**: [Name]
   - Target: [What to mutate]
   - Rationale: [Why this might expose an issue]
   - Expected Issue: [What incorrect behavior might occur]
   
   [Continue for 3-5 test strategies]

{"thoughts": "# Phase 1 Analysis: LoopVectorize Fix for Versioned Strides Propagation

## 1. Fix Understanding

The fix addresses an issue in LoopVectorize where versioned stride values (constant strides guaranteed by predicate scalar evolution) are not properly propagated to users through sign/zero extensions (sext/zext). 

**Key changes:**
1. **Before fix**: Only replaced VPValues for stride values used directly in the loop.
2. **After fix**: Now also handles cases where the stride value is used through sext/zext instructions by:
   - Iterating through all users of the stride value
   - Checking if any user is a SExtInst or ZExtInst
   - If such a user exists and has a corresponding VPValue in the plan, replace its uses with a new constant live-in
   - The new constant is created with the stride value sign-extended to match the extension type

**Context**: The code is part of `tryToBuildVPlanWithVPRecipes` in LoopVectorize.cpp, which processes symbolic strides collected by LoopAccessAnalysis. These strides are loop-invariant values that appear in memory access patterns like `a[i*stride]`. The vectorizer can version these strides (assume they're constant, often 1) to enable better vectorization.

## 2. Assumptions Identified

1. **Assumption A**: Only SExtInst and ZExtInst users need special handling. The fix assumes that if a stride value flows through other instructions before being used in the loop, those intermediate instructions will already have proper VPValues.

2. **Assumption B**: The stride value is always an integer type that can be sign-extended. The fix uses `getSExtValue()` on the stride's APInt, which assumes the stride fits in a signed 64-bit integer.

3. **Assumption C**: The extension instruction's type is compatible with the stride's type. The fix creates a ConstantInt with `U->getType()` using the sign-extended stride value.

4. **Assumption D**: All relevant sext/zext users of the stride will have corresponding VPValues in the plan (`Plan->getLiveIn(U)` returns non-null). The fix skips users without VPValues.

5. **Assumption E**: The stride value is loop-invariant and its SCEV expression simplifies to a constant when predicates are applied (via `PSE.getSCEV(StrideV)`).

6. **Assumption F**: The stride's users are only within the loop being vectorized. The fix doesn't consider users outside the loop (e.g., in LCSSA phi nodes).

7. **Assumption G**: The stride value doesn't flow through multiple levels of extensions or other transformations before being used.

## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

### Case 1: Other Cast Instructions (Breaking Assumption A)
- **Scenario**: The stride value could be used through other cast instructions like `trunc`, `bitcast`, `ptrtoint`, `inttoptr`, or `fpext/fptrunc` for floating-point types.
- **Impact**: These users won't get their VPValues replaced, potentially leading to incorrect code generation.

### Case 2: Large Stride Values (Breaking Assumption B)
- **Scenario**: The stride value is larger than 64 bits or has a value that doesn't fit in int64_t when sign-extended.
- **Impact**: `getSExtValue()` could overflow or produce incorrect results for large APInt values.

### Case 3: Type Incompatibility (Breaking Assumption C)
- **Scenario**: The extension instruction's type has different signedness or bitwidth than expected for the stride value.
- **Impact**: Creating a ConstantInt with the wrong type could lead to incorrect constant propagation or type mismatches.

### Case 4: Missing VPValues for Extended Values (Breaking Assumption D)
- **Scenario**: The sext/zext instruction exists but doesn't have a corresponding VPValue in the plan (e.g., if it's dead code or not part of the vectorized region).
- **Impact**: The fix skips these cases, which might be correct but could miss legitimate cases if VPValue creation is inconsistent.

### Case 5: Complex SCEV Expressions (Breaking Assumption E)
- **Scenario**: The stride's SCEV doesn't simplify to a constant even with predicates, or the simplification is incorrect due to SCEV limitations.
- **Impact**: The versioning might use incorrect constant values.

### Case 6: Outside Loop Users (Breaking Assumption F)
- **Scenario**: The stride value (or its extensions) are used outside the loop, e.g., in LCSSA phi nodes or post-loop computations.
- **Impact**: These users won't get updated VPValues, potentially leading to incorrect live-out values.

### Case 7: Multiple Transformation Levels (Breaking Assumption G)
- **Scenario**: The stride value flows through multiple instructions before being used (e.g., `sext (add (stride, 1))` or `sext (trunc (stride))`).
- **Impact**: Only the immediate sext/zext users are handled, missing indirect uses.

### Case 8: Negative Strides with ZExt (Semantic Issue)
- **Scenario**: A negative stride value used through a `zext` instruction. Using `getSExtValue()` for a zext user could produce incorrect results because zext expects zero-extension, not sign-extension.
- **Impact**: The constant created for the zext user would have the wrong value for negative strides.

### Case 9: Stride Used in Multiple Extensions with Different Types
- **Scenario**: The same stride value is used in both `sext to i64` and `zext to i64` instructions.
- **Impact**: Both would get the same sign-extended constant, which is wrong for the zext case if the stride is negative.

### Case 10: Stride Versioning with Non-Integer Types
- **Scenario**: The stride value has pointer or floating-point type (though unlikely given the context).

{"thoughts": "# Phase 1 Analysis: LoopVectorize Fix for Versioned Strides Propagation

## 1. Fix Understanding

The fix addresses an issue in LoopVectorize where versioned stride values (constant strides guaranteed by predicate scalar evolution) are not properly propagated to users through sign/zero extensions (sext/zext). 

**Key changes:**
1. **Before fix**: Only replaced VPValues for stride values used directly in the loop.
2. **After fix**: Now also handles cases where the stride value is used through sext/zext instructions by:
   - Iterating through all users of the stride value
   - Checking if any user is a SExtInst or ZExtInst
   - If such a user exists and has a corresponding VPValue in the plan, replace its uses with a new constant live-in
   - The new constant is created with the stride value sign-extended to match the extension type

**Context**: The code is part of `tryToBuildVPlanWithVPRecipes` in LoopVectorize.cpp, which processes symbolic strides collected by LoopAccessAnalysis. These strides are loop-invariant values that appear in memory access patterns like `a[i*stride]`. The vectorizer can version these strides (assume they're constant, often 1) to enable better vectorization.

## 2. Assumptions Identified

1. **Assumption A**: Only SExtInst and ZExtInst users need special handling. The fix assumes that if a stride value flows through other instructions before being used in the loop, those intermediate instructions will already have proper VPValues.

2. **Assumption B**: The stride value is always an integer type that can be sign-extended. The fix uses `getSExtValue()` on the stride's APInt, which assumes the stride fits in a signed 64-bit integer.

3. **Assumption C**: The extension instruction's type is compatible with the stride's type. The fix creates a ConstantInt with `U->getType()` using the sign-extended stride value.

4. **Assumption D**: All relevant sext/zext users of the stride will have corresponding VPValues in the plan (`Plan->getLiveIn(U)` returns non-null). The fix skips users without VPValues.

5. **Assumption E**: The stride value is loop-invariant and its SCEV expression simplifies to a constant when predicates are applied (via `PSE.getSCEV(StrideV)`).

6. **Assumption F**: The stride's users are only within the loop being vectorized. The fix doesn't consider users outside the loop (e.g., in LCSSA phi nodes).

7. **Assumption G**: The stride value doesn't flow through multiple levels of extensions or other transformations before being used.

8. **Assumption H**: For ZExtInst users, using `getSExtValue()` is correct (i.e., the stride is non-negative or the semantics don't matter).

## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

### Case 1: Other Cast Instructions (Breaking Assumption A)
- **Scenario**: The stride value could be used through other cast instructions like `trunc`, `bitcast`, `ptrtoint`, `inttoptr`, or `fpext/fptrunc` for floating-point types.
- **Impact**: These users won't get their VPValues replaced, potentially leading to incorrect code generation.

### Case 2: Large Stride Values (Breaking Assumption B)
- **Scenario**: The stride value is larger than 64 bits or has a value that doesn't fit in int64_t when sign-extended.
- **Impact**: `getSExtValue()` could overflow or produce incorrect results for large APInt values.

### Case 3: Type Incompatibility (Breaking Assumption C)
- **Scenario**: The extension instruction's type has different signedness or bitwidth than expected for the stride value.
- **Impact**: Creating a ConstantInt with the wrong type could lead to incorrect constant propagation or type mismatches.

### Case 4: Missing VPValues for Extended Values (Breaking Assumption D)
- **Scenario**: The sext/zext instruction exists but doesn't have a corresponding VPValue in the plan (e.g., if it's dead code or not part of the vectorized region).
- **Impact**: The fix skips these cases, which might be correct but could miss legitimate cases if VPValue creation is inconsistent.

### Case 5: Complex SCEV Expressions (Breaking Assumption E)
- **Scenario**: The stride's SCEV doesn't simplify to a constant even with predicates, or the simplification is incorrect due to SCEV limitations.
- **Impact**: The versioning might use incorrect constant values.

### Case 6: Outside Loop Users (Breaking Assumption F)
- **Scenario**: The stride value (or its extensions) are used outside the loop, e.g., in LCSSA phi nodes or post-loop computations.
- **Impact**: These users won't get updated VPValues, potentially leading to incorrect live-out values.

### Case 7: Multiple Transformation Levels (Breaking Assumption G)
- **Scenario**: The stride value flows through multiple instructions before being used (e.g., `sext (add (stride, 1))` or `sext (trunc (stride))`).
- **Impact**: Only the immediate sext/zext users are handled, missing indirect uses.

### Case 8: Negative Strides with ZExt (Breaking Assumption H)
- **Scenario**: A negative stride value used through a `zext` instruction. Using `getSExtValue()` for a zext user could produce incorrect results because zext expects zero-extension, not sign-extension.
- **Impact**: The constant created for the zext user would have the wrong value for negative strides.

### Case 9: Stride Used in Multiple Extensions with Different Types
- **Scenario**: The same stride value is used in both `sext to i64` and `zext to i64` instructions.
- **Impact**: Both would get the same sign-extended constant, which is wrong for the zext case if the stride is negative.

### Case 10: Stride Versioning with Non-Integer Types
- **Scenario**: The stride value has pointer or floating-point type (though unlikely given the context).

# Phase 2: Generate Verified Test Cases

Here are a list of test cases related to the original fix:

Test Case 0: unknown_inner_stride
Commands:
[["opt < %s -p 'loop-vectorize' -force-vector-interleave=1 -S -force-vector-width=4 -debug-only=loop-accesses,loop-vectorize,loop-utils 2> %t", 'FileCheck %s'], ['cat %t', 'FileCheck %s --check-prefix=DEBUG']]
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-i128:128-n32:64-S128"

define void @unknown_inner_stride(ptr nocapture noundef %dst, ptr nocapture noundef readonly %src, i32 noundef %stride1, i32 noundef %stride2, i32 noundef %m, i32 noundef %n) {
entry:
  %cmp26 = icmp sgt i32 %m, 0
  %cmp224 = icmp sgt i32 %n, 0
  %or.cond = and i1 %cmp26, %cmp224
  br i1 %or.cond, label %outer.loop.preheader, label %exit

outer.loop.preheader:                             ; preds = %entry
  %add6 = add nuw nsw i32 %n, 1
  %0 = sext i32 %stride2 to i64
  %1 = sext i32 %stride1 to i64
  %2 = zext i32 %n to i64
  %3 = zext i32 %add6 to i64
  %wide.trip.count39 = zext i32 %m to i64
  %wide.trip.count = zext i32 %n to i64
  br label %outer.loop

outer.loop:                                       ; preds = %inner.loop.exit, %outer.loop.preheader
  %outer.iv = phi i64 [ 0, %outer.loop.preheader ], [ %outer.iv.next, %inner.loop.exit ]
  %4 = mul nsw i64 %outer.iv, %2
  %5 = mul nsw i64 %outer.iv, %3
  br label %inner.loop

inner.loop:                                       ; preds = %inner.loop, %outer.loop
  %inner.iv = phi i64 [ 0, %outer.loop ], [ %inner.iv.next, %inner.loop ]
  %6 = mul nsw i64 %inner.iv, %0
  %7 = add nsw i64 %6, %4
  %arrayidx.us = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr %src, i64 %7
  %8 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx.us, align 4
  %9 = mul nsw i64 %inner.iv, %1
  %10 = add nsw i64 %9, %5
  %arrayidx11.us = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr %dst, i64 %10
  %11 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx11.us, align 4
  %add12.us = add nsw i32 %11, %8
  store i32 %add12.us, ptr %arrayidx11.us, align 4
  %inner.iv.next = add nuw nsw i64 %inner.iv, 1
  %exitcond.not = icmp eq i64 %inner.iv.next, %wide.trip.count
  br i1 %exitcond.not, label %inner.loop.exit, label %inner.loop

inner.loop.exit:                                  ; preds = %inner.loop
  %outer.iv.next = add nuw nsw i64 %outer.iv, 1
  %exitcond40.not = icmp eq i64 %outer.iv.next, %wide.trip.count39
  br i1 %exitcond40.not, label %exit, label %outer.loop

exit:                                             ; preds = %inner.loop.exit, %entry
  ret void
}


Test Case 0: test_versioned_with_different_uses
Commands:
[['opt -passes=loop-vectorize -force-vector-width=4 -S %s', 'FileCheck %s']]
target datalayout = "e-m:o-p270:32:32-p271:32:32-p272:64:64-i64:64-i128:128-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"

declare i1 @cond()

define void @test_versioned_with_different_uses(i32 %offset, ptr noalias %dst.1, ptr %dst.2) {
entry:
  %offset.ext = zext i32 %offset to i64
  br label %outer.header

outer.header:                                     ; preds = %inner.loop, %entry
  %iv.1 = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %iv.2.next, %inner.loop ]
  %c = call i1 @cond()
  br i1 %c, label %inner.loop, label %exit

inner.loop:                                       ; preds = %inner.loop, %outer.header
  %iv.2 = phi i64 [ %iv.1, %outer.header ], [ %iv.2.next, %inner.loop ]
  %iv.3 = phi i32 [ 0, %outer.header ], [ %iv.3.next, %inner.loop ]
  %iv.mul = mul i32 %iv.3, %offset
  %gep.mul = getelementptr i8, ptr %dst.1, i32 %iv.mul
  store i32 0, ptr %gep.mul, align 8
  %gep = getelementptr i32, ptr %dst.2, i64 %iv.2
  store i32 0, ptr %gep, align 8
  %iv.2.next = add i64 %iv.2, %offset.ext
  %iv.3.next = add i32 %iv.3, 1
  %ec = icmp eq i32 %iv.3, 200
  br i1 %ec, label %outer.header, label %inner.loop

exit:                                             ; preds = %outer.header
  ret void
}

entry:

inner.loop:                                       ; preds = %inner.loop, %outer.header

  %iv.3 = phi i32 [ 0, %outer.header ], [ %iv.3.next, %inner.loop ]
